section.title: The Glory Returns and the Altar
section.sectionRationale: The measured, empty house of chapters 40-42 now receives its purpose: the very glory that departed in stages in chapters 10-11 returns, filling the temple from the east. What sin drove out, grace brings back, and the chapter closes by consecrating the altar where atonement will again be made.
section.thesis: The glory that abandoned a defiled temple now returns to dwell with God's people forever, teaching that God's presence departs from sin but comes back to a purified and consecrated dwelling, a pattern completed when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and consummated when God Himself is the temple of the new creation.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: the glory of the LORD returning and filling the temple; God's declaration that this is the place of His throne where He will dwell forever; Ezekiel commissioned to describe the temple so Israel is ashamed and instructed; the precise measurements of the new altar; and the seven-day rite that consecrates the altar for sacrifice.

units.label: The glory returns
units.note: filling the temple from the east

verses.text: Then the man brought me back to the gate that faces east,
and I saw the {{kavod|glory}} of the God of Israel coming from the east. His voice was like the roar of many waters, and the earth shone with His glory.
The vision I saw was like the vision I had seen when He came to destroy the city and like the visions I had seen by the River Kebar. I fell facedown,
and the glory of the LORD entered the temple through the gate facing east.
Then the Spirit lifted me up and brought me into the inner court, and the glory of the LORD filled the temple.
While the man was standing beside me, I heard someone speaking to me from inside the temple,

and He said to me, “Son of man, this is the place of My throne and the place for the soles of My feet, where I will {{shakan|dwell}} among the Israelites forever. The house of Israel will never again defile My holy name, neither they nor their kings, by their prostitution and by the funeral offerings for their kings at their deaths.

When they placed their threshold next to My threshold and their doorposts beside My doorposts, with only a wall between Me and them, they defiled My holy name by the abominations they committed. Therefore I have consumed them in My anger.
Now let them remove far from Me their prostitution and the funeral offerings for their kings, and I will dwell among them forever.

units.label: Ashamed and instructed: the law of the temple

verses.text: As for you, son of man, describe the temple to the people of Israel, so that they may be ashamed of their iniquities. Let them measure the plan,

and if they are ashamed of all they have done, then make known to them the design of the temple, its arrangement and its exits and entrances, its whole design along with all its statutes, forms, and laws. Write it down in their sight, so that they may keep its complete design and all its statutes and may carry them out.
This is the law of the temple: All its surrounding territory on top of the mountain will be most holy. Yes, this is the law of the temple.

These are the measurements of the altar in long cubits (a cubit and a handbreadth): Its gutter shall be a cubit deep and a cubit wide, with a rim of one span around its edge. And this is the height of the altar:

The space from the gutter on the ground to the lower ledge shall be two cubits, and the ledge one cubit wide. The space from the smaller ledge to the larger ledge shall be four cubits, and the ledge one cubit wide.
The altar hearth shall be four cubits high, and four horns shall project upward from the hearth.
The altar hearth shall be square at its four corners, twelve cubits long and twelve cubits wide.
The ledge shall also be square, fourteen cubits long and fourteen cubits wide, with a rim of half a cubit and a gutter of a cubit all around it. The steps of the altar shall face east.”

Then He said to me: “Son of man, this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘These are the statutes for the altar on the day it is constructed, so that burnt offerings may be sacrificed on it and blood may be sprinkled on it:
You are to give a young bull from the herd as a {{chatat|sin offering}} to the Levitical priests who are of the family of Zadok, who approach Me to minister before Me, declares the Lord GOD.

You are to take some of its blood and put it on the four horns of the altar, on the four corners of the ledge, and all around the rim; thus you will cleanse the altar and make {{kaphar|atonement}} for it.
Then you are to take away the bull for the sin offering and burn it in the appointed part of the temple area outside the sanctuary.
On the second day you are to present an unblemished male goat as a sin offering, and the altar is to be cleansed as it was with the bull.
When you have finished the purification, you are to present a young, unblemished bull and an unblemished ram from the flock.
You must present them before the LORD; the priests are to sprinkle salt on them and sacrifice them as a burnt offering to the LORD.
For seven days you are to provide a male goat daily for a sin offering; you are also to provide a young bull and a ram from the flock, both unblemished.
For seven days the priests are to make atonement for the altar and cleanse it; so they shall consecrate it.
At the end of these days, from the eighth day on, the priests are to present your burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar. Then I will accept you, declares the Lord GOD.’”

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, the visible splendor of God's manifest presence. The very kavod that departed the temple in chapters 10-11 now returns from the east, filling the house exactly where it once stood empty.

shakan.gloss: To dwell, to settle down, to tabernacle. The root behind mishkan, tabernacle; here God promises to shakan, dwell, among His people forever, the goal toward which the whole measured vision has been moving.

chatat.gloss: Sin offering, purification offering. The sacrifice that cleanses the altar itself before it can be used, teaching that even the place of worship needs atonement before a holy God can be approached.

kaphar.gloss: To atone, to cover, to make propitiation. The verb behind Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement; here it describes cleansing the newly built altar itself, so that sacrifice can rightly begin upon it.

basic.context: This chapter is the turning point of the whole temple vision. In chapters 8-11 Ezekiel watched, in stages, the glory of the LORD leave a temple defiled by idolatry, pausing at the threshold before departing over the Mount of Olives. Now, after the measured house of chapters 40-42 stands ready though empty, that same glory returns, from the east, with a voice like many waters, and fills the temple completely. God declares this the place of His throne, promises to dwell there forever if His people put away their defilement, and then gives exact instructions for consecrating the altar so that atonement, and worship, can begin again.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter deliberately recalls Ezekiel's own earlier visions, 'like the vision I had seen when He came to destroy the city,' 43:3, so that readers feel the reversal: the glory that left in judgment now returns in mercy, following the very route it took away. As with the surrounding temple vision, interpreters differ on how literally to press the architectural and ritual detail, but the theological center is unmistakable: sin drives God's presence away, and only genuine repentance and atonement make His dwelling possible again.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is built around kavod, glory, the same word used for the throne vision of chapter 1 and the departing glory of chapters 10-11, now returning. God promises to shakan, dwell, among His people forever, the very root behind the tabernacle's name. And the new altar must be cleansed by a chatat, sin offering, so that kaphar, atonement, can be made before ordinary worship begins.
basic.typology: The glory's return by the east gate anticipates a greater return: the eternal Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us, John 1:14, the same root idea as shakan here. The altar's seven-day consecration by blood anticipates the once-for-all cleansing accomplished at the cross, after which no further consecration of an altar is ever needed.
basic.christ: John deliberately echoes this chapter's language when he writes that 'the Word became flesh and dwelt,' literally tabernacled, 'among us, and we have seen His glory,' John 1:14, the same kavod Ezekiel watched fill this temple. Where Ezekiel needed seven days of sacrifice to consecrate an altar, Hebrews says Christ 'entered once for all into the holy places... by means of His own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption,' Hebrews 9:12. The temple that received the glory back is fulfilled in the One who is Himself God with us, and who will one day be, with the Lamb, the temple of the new creation, Revelation 21:22-23.
basic.god: God is holy and cannot dwell amid ongoing defilement; His anger in chapters 8-11 was real and just. Yet He is a God whose settled intention is to dwell with His people, not to remain at a distance; the very first thing He says upon returning is a promise, 'I will dwell among them forever' (43:9), conditioned only on their putting away what drove Him out.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 10:18-19 and 11:22-23 describe the glory's earlier, grieving departure from this same temple and city, now reversed. John 1:14 identifies the same glory, tabernacling among us, in Christ. Hebrews 9:11-12 fulfills the altar's repeated consecration in Christ's once-for-all entrance by His own blood. Revelation 21:3 and 22 show God's dwelling finally and fully with man.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story keeps circling one question: will God dwell with His people, or must He depart because of sin? Eden lost that presence; the tabernacle and temple recovered it in shadow; sin drove the glory out in Ezekiel's own lifetime; and here, that same glory returns, pointing beyond itself to Christ, in whom God's presence became permanent and unshakable, and to the new creation, where God dwells with His people forever, needing no temple at all because He is the temple.

context.literary: The hinge of the entire temple vision, and in many ways of the whole book: the empty, measured house of chapters 40-42 finally receives the presence it was built for, in language that deliberately mirrors and reverses the departure scenes of chapters 8-11 and the inaugural vision of chapter 1.
context.historical: For exiles who had watched, through Ezekiel's earlier visions, the unthinkable, God's own glory abandoning His temple and city, this chapter answers with an equally vivid reversal: the glory returning by the very gate it once departed, promising a permanent, unbroken dwelling this time.
context.canonical: This return of the glory stands as one of Scripture's great turning points, directly informing John's language of the Word tabernacling among us, John 1:14, and anticipating the final, unmediated dwelling of God with His people in Revelation 21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce, in the most vivid terms available, that the glory which departed in judgment has returned in mercy, conditioned on Israel's genuine shame over past defilement and the fresh consecration of a holy altar.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific prophetic vision and its accompanying instructions for a future altar; it is not a manual to be mechanically re-enacted verse by verse today. Its abiding truth is theological: God's presence departs from persistent sin but returns to a people and a place made holy through atonement.

debates.question: Why does Ezekiel stress that this vision is 'like the vision... by the River Kebar' (43:3)?

views.label: To assure continuity of identity
views.case: The same God, the same glory, appears again, so that exiles need not doubt this is a genuine return of the LORD Himself rather than merely a new or different vision.

views.label: To highlight a deliberate reversal
views.case: The vision that once executed judgment on the city, chapters 9-10, has become the vision that graciously restores it, so that the very language of departure is now used for homecoming.

debates.bearing: Both readings work together: the same glory, faithfully identified, now moves in the opposite direction, from judgment to mercy.

debates.question: Do the altar and its sacrifices in 43:18-27 anticipate a future, literal reinstitution of animal offerings?

views.label: Yes, a future literal altar and offerings
views.case: Some read this as describing sacrifices to be literally reinstituted at a future temple, understood as commemorative or covenantal rather than atoning in the fullest sense, since Christ's work is already accomplished.

views.label: A typological pattern fulfilled in Christ
views.case: Others read these details as pointing to, and finding their complete terminus in, Christ's once-for-all sacrifice, since Hebrews teaches that His single offering ended the need for repeated sacrifices, Hebrews 10:1-14.

debates.bearing: Interpretive humility is warranted, but the New Testament's clear teaching that Christ's sacrifice is final and fully sufficient should shape how any reader understands the offerings pictured here.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on kavod, glory, the same word used of the throne vision in chapter 1 and the grieving departure of chapters 10-11, now flooding back into the temple. God promises to shakan, dwell, among His people forever, the very root behind mishkan, tabernacle. And the new altar must first receive a chatat, a sin offering, so that kaphar, atonement, can be made, before ordinary worship is ever offered upon it.

typology.type: The glory's departure and return by the east gate
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us, John 1:14, God's presence no longer departing but permanently dwelling with His people in Christ.

typology.type: The altar consecrated through seven days of sin offerings and atoning blood
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ's once-for-all sacrifice, which secures an eternal redemption and ends the need for repeated consecration, Hebrews 9:12; 10:1-14.

study.christ: John deliberately echoes this chapter's language when he writes that 'the Word became flesh and dwelt,' literally tabernacled, 'among us, and we have seen His glory,' John 1:14, the same kavod Ezekiel watched fill this temple. Where Ezekiel needed seven days of sacrifice to consecrate an altar, Hebrews says Christ 'entered once for all into the holy places... by means of His own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption,' Hebrews 9:12. The temple that received the glory back is fulfilled in the One who is Himself God with us, and who will one day be, with the Lamb, the temple of the new creation, Revelation 21:22-23.
study.god: God is holy and cannot dwell amid ongoing defilement, His anger in chapters 8-11 was real and just. Yet He is a God whose settled intention is to dwell with His people, not to remain at a distance; the very first thing He says upon returning is a promise, 'I will dwell among them forever' (43:9), conditioned only on their putting away what drove Him out.

crossReferences.note: The glory's earlier departure from the temple threshold, now reversed as it returns by the same gate.
The glory finally leaves the city over the Mount of Olives, the very route this vision's return reverses.
The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, the same glory now seen fully in Christ.
God's dwelling is finally with man, and the new Jerusalem needs no temple, for God and the Lamb are its temple.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story keeps circling one question: will God dwell with His people, or must He depart because of sin? Eden lost that presence; the tabernacle and temple recovered it in shadow; sin drove the glory out in Ezekiel's own lifetime; and here, that same glory returns, pointing beyond itself to Christ, in whom God's presence became permanent and unshakable, and to the new creation, where God dwells with His people forever, needing no temple at all because He is the temple.

quiz.q: From which direction does the glory of the LORD return to the temple?

options.text: From the west, over the Mediterranean
options.note: The text specifies a different direction.

options.text: From the north, through the idol gate
options.note: The idol gate is significant elsewhere in Ezekiel's visions, but the glory here returns from the east.

options.text: The text does not specify a direction
options.note: 43:2 and 43:4 are explicit that the glory comes from, and enters through, the east.

options.text: From the east, the same direction from which it had departed
options.note: 43:2 and 43:4 state this clearly, reversing the earlier departure over the Mount of Olives to the east.

quiz.q: What does God call this place in 43:7?

options.text: A monument to Israel's disobedience
options.note: The emphasis is on future dwelling and promise, not a monument to past failure.

options.text: The place of My throne and the place for the soles of My feet, where I will dwell forever
options.note: 43:7 records this declaration in almost these exact words.

options.text: A place He refuses to dwell in again
options.note: The opposite is stated; God promises to dwell there forever.

options.text: A temporary resting place until the next exile
options.note: The promise given is permanence, 'forever,' not a temporary arrangement.

quiz.q: What is Ezekiel commanded to do in 43:10-11?

options.text: Keep the vision completely secret from the people
options.note: He is told to describe it to the people, not conceal it.

options.text: Destroy the vision's plans because Israel is unworthy
options.note: He is told to record and make known the design, not destroy it.

options.text: Build the temple with his own hands immediately
options.note: He is commanded to describe and record the design, not to build it himself.

options.text: Describe the temple's design to Israel so that they may be ashamed of their iniquities and keep its statutes
options.note: 43:10-11 gives this exact commission.

quiz.q: What must happen to the altar before it can be used for regular offerings?

options.text: It must be moved to a different location outside the temple
options.note: No relocation is described; it must be cleansed in place.

options.text: It must be left unused for seven years
options.note: The consecration period given is seven days, not seven years.

options.text: It must be cleansed and consecrated over seven days through sin offerings and atoning blood
options.note: 43:18-27 describes this seven-day consecration in detail.

options.text: Nothing; it is ready for use immediately upon completion
options.note: The text describes a required seven-day process of cleansing before ordinary use begins.

quiz.q: What does God promise conditionally in 43:9, if Israel removes its former defilement?

options.text: That He will send them into exile one more time
options.note: The promise given is dwelling, not further exile.

options.text: That He will withdraw and never return
options.note: The whole chapter is about His return and promised presence, the opposite of withdrawal.

options.text: That He will destroy the temple again
options.note: The chapter's entire momentum is toward permanent dwelling, not further destruction.

options.text: That He will dwell among them forever
options.note: 43:9 states this promise directly, conditioned on removing the former defilement.

questions: The glory returns by the very gate it once departed through. Where in your own life might you need to trust that God can return to, and restore, places you thought were permanently lost to sin?

God says He will dwell with His people 'forever' only once they remove what defiled His name (43:9). How does the gospel's promise of full acceptance in Christ meet, rather than remove, the call to genuine repentance?

Ezekiel is told to describe the temple so Israel would be 'ashamed of their iniquities' (43:10). Is there a place where a clearer picture of God's holiness, rather than mere information, would produce healthy shame and real change in you?

The altar itself needed seven days of cleansing before it could be used for ordinary worship. How does knowing that even the place of sacrifice needed atonement deepen your appreciation for Christ's finished, once-for-all sacrifice?

John says Christ 'tabernacled' among us, using the very word behind God's promise to dwell with Israel here. How does picturing Jesus as this chapter's fulfillment change the way you approach Him in worship this week?

translationNotes.note: 'The vision I saw was like the vision I had seen when He came to destroy the city' deliberately links this scene back to the departure visions of chapters 8-11 and the inaugural vision of chapter 1, using closely parallel language so the reader recognizes the very same glory now returning.

'The soles of My feet' is a striking image, likely describing the temple, or the ark within it, as God's footstool, a picture used elsewhere for the earthly point of contact with a heavenly throne, compare 1 Chronicles 28:2 and Psalm 132:7.

'The Levitical priests of the family of Zadok' repeats a qualification given earlier in the vision, 40:46, restricting altar service to a specific priestly line, a detail read by some as a literal future arrangement and by others as a picture of a fully qualified, wholly consecrated priesthood.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 43:1-27. Em dashes in 43:7 and 43:11 replaced with commas; an embedded line break in the middle of verse 13 flattened into continuous prose. No em or en dashes used. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found addressing this passage directly this session, so sermon status is none.
